questions.prompt_text: Provide an appropriate response. Suppose the velocity of a body moving along the s-axis is (ds/dt) = 9.8t - 6. Is it necessary to know the initial position of the body to find the body's displacement over some time interval? Justify your answer.

choices.label: A
choices.html: Yes,  knowing the initial position is the only way to find the exact positions at the beginning and end of the time interval.  Those positions are needed to find the displacement.
choices.text: Yes, knowing the initial position is the only way to find the exact positions at the beginning and end of the time interval. Those positions are needed to find the displacement.

choices.label: B
choices.html: No, displacement has nothing to do with the position of the body.
choices.text: No, displacement has nothing to do with the position of the body.

choices.label: C
choices.html: No, the initial position is necessary to find the curve s= f(t) but not necessary to find the displacement.  The initial position determines the integration constant.  When finding the displacement the integration constant is subtracted out.
choices.text: No, the initial position is necessary to find the curve s= f(t) but not necessary to find the displacement. The initial position determines the integration constant. When finding the displacement the integration constant is subtracted out.

choices.label: D
choices.html: Yes, integration is not possible without knowing the initial position.
choices.text: Yes, integration is not possible without knowing the initial position.

questions.prompt_html: Evaluate  dx.

questions.solution_html:
First, let us rewrite the integral in the form 
 dx =  (2x dx)
Let u = x2; hence du = 2x dx.
Substituting into the integral above, we obtain  du = sin-1 + C ( From Table)
Recall u = x2, hence  dx = sin-1 + C.
questions.solution_text: First, let us rewrite the integral in the form indefinite integral of ((2x/square root of (9 - (x) with superscript (4)))) dx = indefinite integral of ((1/square root of (9 - (((x) with superscript (2))) with superscript (2)))) (2x dx) Let u = x 2 ; hence du = 2x dx. questions.prompt_html:
The Mean-Value Theorem for integrals asserts that: If f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists a point c in [ a, b] such that  dx = (b - a) f(c).
Verify that the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied to the function f(x) =   
on the interval [a, b] = [0, 3], and find the point c guaranteed by the Theorem.
questions.prompt_text: The Mean-Value Theorem for integrals asserts that: If f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists a point c in [ a, b] such that integral of (f(x)) from (a) to (b) dx = (b - a) f(c). Verify that the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied to the function f(x) = (x/square root of (16 + (x) with superscript (2))) on the interval [a, b] = [0, 3], and find the point c guaranteed by the Theorem.
questions.solution_html:
First, observe that the function f(x) =  is continuous on the interval [0, 3]. Therefore the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied. Let us compute the left and right hand sides of the equation separately.
Right Hand Side: (b - a) f(c) = ( 3 - 0)  = 
Left Hand Side: I = dx = dx.
We shall use the substitution u = 16 + x2, hence du = 2x dx or x dx =  du.
Note also that at x = 0, u = 16, and at x = 3, u = 16 + 9 = 25. We obtain 
  I =  du =  =  -  = 5 - 4 = 1
Equating left and right hand sides, we obtain: 1 =  or  = 3c.
  To solve for c, let us square both sides to get 16 + c2 = 9c2 .  
It follows that 8c2 = 16, hence c = +  ( since c > 0 ).
questions.solution_text: First, observe that the function f(x) = (x/square root of (16 + (x) with superscript (2))) is continuous on the interval [0, 3]. Therefore the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied. Let us compute the left and right hand sides of the equation separately. Right Hand Side: (b - a) f(c) = ( 3 - 0) (c/square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2))) = (3c/square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2))) Left Hand Side: I = integral of (f(x)) from (a) to (b) dx = integral of ((x/square root of (16 + (x) with superscript (2)))) from (0) to (3) dx. We shall use the substitution u = 16 + x 2 , hence du = 2x dx or x dx = (1/2) du. Note also that at x = 0, u = 16, and at x = 3, u = 16 + 9 = 25. To solve for c, let us square both sides to get 16 + c 2 = 9c 2 . It follows that 8c 2 = 16, hence c = + square root of (2 ) ( since c > 0 ).

questions.prompt_html: Use the half-angle substitution x = tan (θ/2) to evaluate dθ.
questions.prompt_text: Use the half-angle substitution x = tan (θ/2) to evaluate indefinite integral of ((1/1 + sin(θ) + cos(θ)) ) dθ.
questions.solution_html:
Recall if x =tan(θ/2), then sin(θ) = , cos (θ) =  and dθ = dx
Substituting into the given integral, we obtain :
dθ =   dx =  dx
Simplifying we get  = ln|1 + x| + C.
Substituting x = tan(θ/2), we obtain dθ = ln|1 + tan(0/2)| + C.
questions.solution_text: Recall if x =tan(θ/2), then sin(θ) = (2x/1 + (x) with superscript (2)) , cos (θ) = (1 - (x) with superscript (2 )/1 + (x) with superscript (2)) and dθ = (2/1 + (x) with superscript (2)) dx Substituting into the given integral, we obtain : indefinite integral of ((1/1 + sin(θ) + cos(θ)) ) dθ = indefinite integral of ((1/(1 + (2x/1 + (x) with superscript (2)) + (1 - (x) with superscript (2 )/1 + (x) with superscript (2))) )) (2/1 + (x) with superscript (2)) dx = indefinite integral of ((2/1 + (x) with superscript (2 )+ 2x +1 - (x) with superscript (2) )) dx Simplifying we get indefinite integral of ((dx/1+x)) = ln|1 + x| + C. Substituting x = tan(θ/2), we obtain indefinite integral of ((1/1 + sin(θ) + cos(θ)) ) dθ = ln|1 + tan(0/2)| + C.

feedback_html.solution:
Recall if x =tan(θ/2), then sin(θ) = , cos (θ) =  and dθ = dx
Substituting into the given integral, we obtain :
dθ =   dx =  dx
Simplifying we get  = ln|1 + x| + C.
Substituting x = tan(θ/2), we obtain dθ = ln|1 + tan(0/2)| + C.
